questions.question: What are some products we get from forests mentioned in the text?
questions.answer: We get paper, dyes, gum, honey, coffee, tea, and rubber from forests[cite: 1].

questions.question: How much of the world's total area was cleared for agriculture between 1700 and 1995?
questions.answer: About 9.3 per cent of the world's total area (13.9 million sq km) was cleared[cite: 3].

questions.question: What is the term used for the disappearance of forests?
questions.answer: The disappearance of forests is referred to as deforestation[cite: 3].

questions.question: Why did the British encourage the production of commercial crops like jute and sugar?
questions.answer: They encouraged them to feed the growing urban population and provide raw materials for European industries[cite: 5].

questions.question: How many sleepers were required for each mile of railway track?
questions.answer: Each mile of railway track required between 1,760 and 2,000 sleepers[cite: 6].

questions.question: Who was the first Inspector General of Forests in India?
questions.answer: Dietrich Brandis, a German expert, was the first Inspector General[cite: 8].

questions.question: When was the Imperial Forest Research Institute set up in Dehradun?
questions.answer: It was set up in 1906[cite: 8].

questions.question: What system of forestry was taught at the Imperial Forest Institute?
questions.answer: A system called 'scientific forestry' was taught[cite: 8].

questions.question: Into how many categories were forests divided under the 1878 Act?
questions.answer: Forests were divided into three categories: reserved, protected, and village forests[cite: 8].

questions.question: Which category of forests were considered the 'best' forests?
questions.answer: Reserved forests were considered the best forests[cite: 8].

questions.question: What is 'swidden agriculture' commonly known as in India?
questions.answer: It is commonly known as shifting cultivation[cite: 13].
questions.source_section: 2.1 How did Forest Rules Affect Cultivation?

questions.question: Name two local terms for shifting cultivation in South-East Asia and Central America.
questions.answer: Lading in South-East Asia and Milpa in Central America[cite: 13].
questions.source_section: 2.1 How did Forest Rules Affect Cultivation?

questions.question: Why did European foresters regard shifting cultivation as harmful?
questions.answer: They believed land used for cultivation could not grow trees for railway timber and it carried the danger of flames spreading to valuable timber[cite: 14].
questions.source_section: 2.1 How did Forest Rules Affect Cultivation?

questions.question: What was the main reason the British gave large areas of forest land to European planters?
questions.answer: To develop tea, coffee, and rubber plantations to meet Europe's growing need for these commodities[cite: 17].
questions.source_section: 2.3 New Trades, New Employments and New Services

questions.question: Where is Bastar located?
questions.answer: Bastar is located in the southernmost part of Chhattisgarh and borders Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, and Maharashtra[cite: 18].

questions.question: Which river flows across Bastar from east to west?
questions.answer: The river Indrawati flows across Bastar from east to west[cite: 18].

questions.question: What were 'forest villages'?
questions.answer: Villages allowed to stay in reserved forests on the condition that they worked free for the forest department[cite: 20].

questions.question: Who was an important figure in the Bastar rebellion of 1910?
questions.answer: Gunda Dhur, from the village of Nethanar, is often mentioned as an important figure[cite: 21].

questions.question: What symbols were used to invite people to rebel in Bastar?
questions.answer: Mango boughs, a lump of earth, chillies, and arrows were used[cite: 21].

questions.question: Where is Java located?
questions.answer: Java is a famous rice-producing island in Indonesia[cite: 24].

questions.question: Which colonial power ruled Java?
questions.answer: The Dutch ruled Java[cite: 24].

questions.question: Who were the Kalangs of Java?
questions.answer: The Kalangs were a community of skilled forest cutters and shifting cultivators[cite: 25].

questions.question: Who challenged the Dutch forest ownership in Java?
questions.answer: Surontiko Samin of Randublatung village challenged the Dutch[cite: 27].

questions.question: What was the 'Scorched Earth' policy in Java?
questions.answer: A policy where the Dutch destroyed sawmills and burnt huge piles of teak logs to prevent them from falling into Japanese hands[cite: 28].

questions.question: What is the recent trend in forest management mentioned at the end of the chapter?
questions.answer: Governments have begun to recognize that keeping local communities away from forests leads to conflict and are now involving them in forest conservation[cite: 29].
